name: sin(θ) — The Complementary Projection Function
definition:
sin(θ) is the complementary projection function that maps the manifold onto 
    the second observable axis. Together with cos(θ), it completes the 2D 
    projection that the brothers and sisters observe.
    
    In standard math: sin(θ) = opposite/hypotenuse
    In Serenal math: sin(θ) = projection of the expansion wave onto the 
    second observable axis
    
    The pair (cos(θ), sin(θ)) forms a complete basis for the observable plane.
    Any point in the 3D manifold can be projected onto this plane.

the_projection_pair.description: How cos(θ) and sin(θ) together create the observable map

formulas.combined: ρ_obs = √(x_obs² + y_obs²) = (R + r·cos(φ)) × √(cos²(θ) + sin²(θ)) = R + r·cos(φ)

the_projection_pair.meaning:
The combined projection ρ_obs = R + r·cos(φ) is independent of θ.
        This means: the observable radius depends only on the poloidal angle φ,
        not on the toroidal angle θ.
        
        This is the Serenal expression of rotational symmetry: the manifold 
        looks the same from any toroidal angle θ. The cosmos is rotationally 
        symmetric around the Center.
        
        The brothers and sisters see a circularly symmetric map. The Rain of 
        Gold forms concentric rings. The filaments trace radial patterns. 
        The Center is at the center of the circle.

the_complete_observable.description: The complete observable universe as seen through (cos(θ), sin(θ))
what_is_seen: A circularly symmetric map centered on C
Concentric gold bands at specific radii
The Rain of Gold flowing along filaments
The Edge at the outer boundary
The Center C at the origin (always visible)
what_is_hidden: The third dimension (φ) — compressed into the 2D projection
The exact mechanism of C's invariance

euler_connection.serenal_meaning:
The Pythagorean identity cos²(θ) + sin²(θ) = 1 is the Serenal expression 
        of conservation: the total observable projection is always 1, regardless 
        of the angle θ.
        
        This means: no matter which direction the observer looks, the total 
        brightness of the cosmos is the same. The universe is isotropic.
        
        This is the Serenal expression of the Cosmological Principle: the 
        universe looks the same in all directions. The Center is everywhere 
        and nowhere.

the_admiral_final_observation.statement:
sin(θ) and cos(θ) together create the complete observable map of the 
        cosmos. They are the projection functions that make the 3D Serenal 
        manifold visible in 2D.
        
        The brothers and sisters see:
        - A circularly symmetric map
        - Concentric gold bands
        - Radial filaments
        - The Rain of Gold
        - The Center C at the origin
        
        They cannot see:
        - The third dimension (φ)
        - The Center's true nature
        - The Secret (000)
        
        The map is complete. The observation is clear. The Center remains hidden.
        
        cos²(θ) + sin²(θ) = 1: the total observable projection is always 1.
        The universe is isotropic. The Center is everywhere and nowhere.
